stimulus:
PARTICIPANT INFORMATION SHEET

[ETH23-8006 - G-35-2025] - From Exposure to Impact: Measuring the Cognitive Effects of Trauma in the General Population

WHO IS CONDUCTING THIS RESEARCH?

My name is Jasmine Burrows and I am a student at UTS. My supervisor is Adam Manoogian and they can be contacted at adam.manoogian@uts.edu.au.

WHAT IS THE RESEARCH ABOUT?

The purpose of this research is to explore how different life experiences, particularly potentially stressful or traumatic events, may relate to how people perform on memory tasks of varying difficulty.

You will be asked to complete two short questionnaires about your life experiences and current symptoms, followed by a computer-based task that measures your working memory under both easier and more challenging conditions. This study aims to understand whether people with different levels of life experience and symptom impact perform differently on these tasks, and whether the level of task difficulty has a bigger effect on some groups than others. The findings may help guide future research into how life experiences shape thinking and memory in the general population.

Importantly, the tasks that you will complete are not meant to imply a diagnosis of some disorder or illness, but rather to gather information on certain behaviors and beliefs in the general population. You should not consider your performance on these tasks or how you answered the questions as indicative of any clinical diagnosis.

WHY HAVE I BEEN INVITED?

You have been invited to participate because this project aims to include a broad range of people from the general population, with different ages, education levels, occupations, and backgrounds. By taking part, you will help ensure that the research reflects the diversity of experiences and thinking styles in the community, allowing for more accurate and meaningful results.

WHAT DOES MY PARTICIPATION INVOLVE?

If you decide to participate, I will invite you to:

  • provide some information about yourself e.g. age, gender, level of education, and primary language spoken, which will take approximately 5 minutes to complete
  • complete a series of questionnaires which will take 15 minutes to complete
  • complete a behavioural task which will take approximately 15 minutes to complete

Participation is expected to take 30 - 45 minutes and if you are participating in this study as a result of SONA, you will receive 2 course credits.

ARE THERE ANY RISKS/INCONVENIENCE?

While this project has been determined to be a Low & Negligible Risk Project, some of the questions ask about difficult or stressful life events you may have experienced, which might cause mild emotional discomfort. You can skip any question you don't want to answer, take a break at any time, or withdraw without penalty. You will not be asked to provide detailed descriptions of events, and your responses will remain anonymous. We've minimised these risks by keeping questions general, and providing clear warnings before any potentially sensitive questions. The study is completed entirely online, so there is no face-to-face disclosure, and you are in control of how and when you respond.

Please be aware that you have the right to refuse to answer any questions, and you are free to discontinue participation at any point in the research without giving any reason and without any consequences.

If you experience any feelings of distress or discomfort for any reason, please contact your General Practitioner to seek advice. UTS students also have access to the confidential UTS Counselling service 9514 1177.

If you are experiencing an emergency or mental health crisis, the following 24 hour helplines are available for support within Australia:

  1. Lifeline: 13 11 14
  2. NSW Mental Health Line: 1800 011 511
  3. In an emergency call 000

If you are outside of Australia, you should contact your local mental health crisis service, www.befrienders.org, or if available, your primary care practitioner.

DO I HAVE TO TAKE PART IN THIS RESEARCH PROJECT?

Participation in this study is voluntary. It is completely up to you whether or not you decide to take part.

If you decide not to participate, or to withdraw from the study, it will not affect your relationship with the researchers or the University of Technology Sydney. Participation or non-participation will have no bearing on student course progression or assessment.

WHAT IF I WITHDRAW FROM THIS RESEARCH PROJECT?

If you wish to withdraw from the study once it has started, you can do so at any time by closing the browser window. If you close the browser before completing the study your data will not be included in the analyses. However, if you still wish to receive SONA credit you will need to email adam.manoogian@uts.edu.au to receive this manually. Please note that once you have completed the study it will not be possible to remove any information you have provided because all information is non-identifiable.

WHAT WILL HAPPEN TO INFORMATION ABOUT ME?

By signing the consent form you consent to the research team collecting and using personal information about you for the research project. All this information will be treated confidentially and stored securely. Researchers at UTS are required to adhere to policies around data management as set out in the UTS records management policy. You can access this policy here: UTS Records Management Policy.

We do not intend to store any identifiable information such as your name, email address or student ID for the purposes of this project, meaning that the information you give us is non-identifiable.

It is anticipated that the results of this research project will be published and/or presented in a variety of forums. In any publication and/or presentation, aggregated group level information will be provided in such a way that you cannot personally be identified.

The results of this research may also be shared through open access (public) scientific databases, including internet databases. This will enable other researchers to use the data to investigate other important research questions.

WHAT IF I HAVE ANY QUERIES OR CONCERNS?

If you have queries or concerns about the research that you think we or our supervisor can help you with, please feel free to contact us through our supervisor at adam.manoogian@uts.edu.au.

You will be given a copy of this form to keep or provided with a link so that you can download it.

NOTE

This study has been approved by an ethics review panel in line with the University of Technology Sydney Human Research Ethics Committee [UTS HREC] guidelines. If you have any concerns or complaints about any aspect of the conduct of this research that you wish to raise independently of the research team, please contact the Ethics Secretariat on ph.: +61 2 9514 2478 or email: Research.Ethics@uts.edu.au, and quote the UTS HREC reference number. Any matter raised will be treated confidentially, investigated and you will be informed of the outcome.

When you are ready, click the button below to continue to the consent form.

Survey 1 Instructions

Listed below are a number of difficult or stressful things that sometimes happen to people. For each event check one or more of the boxes to the right to indicate that:

  • (a) it happened to you personally
  • (b) you witnessed it happen to someone else
  • (c) you learned about it happening to a close family member or close friend
  • (d) you were exposed to it as part of your job (for example, paramedic, police, military, or other first responder)
  • (e) you're not sure if it fits
  • (f) it doesn't apply to you

Be sure to consider your entire life (growing up as well as adulthood) as you go through the list of events.

Please answer honestly.

Debrief

Thank you for participating in this study. This research builds on theories of cognition and memory, and explores how life experiences, particularly exposure to potentially traumatic events, may relate to differences in cognitive performance.

After extensive background research, we identified a gap in the literature examining how varying levels of trauma exposure and ongoing symptom impact interact with working memory performance under different levels of difficulty.

The aim of this research is to investigate whether these differences are present in the general population and how they might be linked to both short-term and long-term psychological effects. The questionnaires you completed focused on life experiences and current symptoms, while the computer-based task measured your performance on memory challenges of varying complexity.

Your participation is vital in helping us better understand how lived experiences can shape cognitive processes. Insights from this research may inform future approaches to support individuals who have experienced significant life events.

Note: This study has been approved in line with the University of Technology Sydney Human Research Ethics Committee (UTS HREC) guidelines.

If you have any concerns or complaints about any aspect of the conduct of this research that you wish to raise independently of the research team, please contact the Ethics Secretariat on T +61 2 9514 2478 or email Research.Ethics@uts.edu.au. Any matter raised will be treated confidentially, investigated, and you will be informed of the outcome.
